fact: MLFQ uses 3 priority queues: Q0 Interactive, Q1 Sub-agent, Q2 Background
Zombie reaper scans every 5 seconds
Zombie threshold: 30 seconds hang time
Probabilistic recovery: 50% success rate on retry
MLFQ inspired by CTSS (Corbato 1962) and Linux CFS (Molnar 2007)
Priority boosting inspired by Solaris TS scheduling and CFS vruntime
Rate limit handling: token bucket per model API, AIMD backoff, admission control
Fairness via DRF from YARN (Vavilapalli 2013, Ghodsi 2011)
Normal scenario: 27 turns, 3 agents, 5% hang rate, AgentRM P95=4495ms, zombies=0
High Load: 280 turns, 10 agents, 10% hang, AgentRM P95=323001ms, zombies=7, recovered=22
Faulty: 63 turns, 5 agents, 30% hang, AgentRM P95=77524ms, zombies=5, recovered=15, throughput=11.0
Cascade: 149 turns, 5 agents, 5-40% oscillating hang, AgentRM zombies=4, recovered=21
Burst: 30 turns in 3s, 8% hang, AgentRM zombies=0, recovered=2
OpenClaw #12442: 6+ hours blocking, 6+ unresponsive episodes in 12 hours
OpenClaw #25992: zombie subagents held lanes 11+ minutes
OpenClaw #3181: runaway heartbeat loop caused rate limit cascade
Scheduler uses O(log n) priority queues, semaphore-controlled lane pool
Work-conserving: lends idle resources from high-priority to low-priority tasks
Abstract claims 86% P95 reduction and 168% throughput increase - these match faulty scenario
Introduction claims 0 vs 27 zombies and 45.2 vs 45.1 throughput - not directly found in tables
